food_name: Curry Puff (Old Chang Kee Style, Box of 10)

ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry, filled, fried — closest parent category for fried filled pastry snacks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Singapore curry puff; A0CKP (fried filled pastry) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for curry puff as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Old Chang Kee-style curry puff weighs approximately 65 g; a box of 10 provides roughly 650 g total, with a typical single-serve portion being one to two puffs.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Puff (Karipap) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy composite estimate for fried filled pastry
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists curry puff (karipap) per 100 g. Values cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fried pastry entries. Old Chang Kee Curry'O is a commercial variant; nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Foodo curry puff entry as the closest approved Singaporean source. Minor adjustments for fat content reflect the deep-fried shell typical of Old Chang Kee products.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat-flour fried pastry / savoury puff — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried wheat-flour pastry products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Old Chang Kee Curry'O or generic Singapore curry puff in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entries: fried wheat-flour savoury pastry products in the University of Sydney GI Database cluster around GI 50–60. A GI of 55 is classified as 'low'. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 21.3) / 100 ≈ 11.7, rounded to 11.8. Serving carbohydrate based on 65 g portion × 32.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 21.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~247 mg per puff)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit curry puff consumption to one puff per occasion, as the sodium content from seasoned filling and pastry can contribute meaningfully to the recommended daily sodium ceiling of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) and moderate carbohydrate load from wheat-flour pastry shell
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat curry puffs as an occasional snack, limiting to one puff per serving and pairing with a low-GI food or protein source to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.2 g per 100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g per 100 g) from deep-frying and pastry shortening
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit curry puff intake due to the saturated and trans fat content from the deep-fried pastry shell, which can adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Singapore curry puff (karipap) is a deep-fried savoury pastry snack that is one of the most iconic street foods in Singapore. Old Chang Kee's Curry'O is the commercial flagship variant, sold in boxes of 10 (with promotional free puffs). The pastry shell is made from wheat flour dough that is deep-fried to a golden, flaky, and crispy exterior. The filling typically consists of spiced curried potato, onion, and either chicken or egg, seasoned with curry powder, turmeric, and salt. Each puff weighs approximately 65 g. Nutritionally, a single puff provides roughly 194 kcal, 10.7 g fat, 21.3 g carbohydrate, and 4.0 g protein. The low glycemic index (~55) reflects the wheat-flour pastry base. Sodium content is moderate at ~380 mg per 100 g. The saturated fat content (~7.2 g per 100 g) is clinically relevant for cardiovascular and lipid management. Curry puffs are a common snack and light meal in Singapore, Malaysia, and across Southeast Asia, often consumed at breakfast, tea-time, or as a quick on-the-go bite. They are generally halal-certified when produced by Old Chang Kee. The dish has Malay, Indian, and British colonial culinary influences, reflecting Singapore's multicultural food heritage.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a curry puff?
Old Chang Kee curry puff nutrition
How much fat in a curry puff?
Is Old Chang Kee curry puff halal?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo curry puff (karipap) entry, which is the closest approved Singaporean primary source. Old Chang Kee Curry'O is a specific commercial product; exact branded nutritional data is not available in any approved primary source database. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried wheat-flour pastry entries. Confidence is medium rather than high because exact product-level data is unavailable.
notes: vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo curry puff source entry for this food category. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source for this food. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional field survey listing 'Curry'O Box of 10 (Free Curry'O x 2)' to the canonical English food name 'Curry Puff (Old Chang Kee Style, Box of 10)'. Per-100g nutrient values represent a single curry puff unit; the box-of-10 format is a retail packaging unit. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 55 is classified as 'low'. Trans fat value of 0.2 g per 100 g is an estimate based on typical deep-fried wheat-flour pastry products; the HPB source does not explicitly report trans fat for this item.
